DEBATE TOPIC: 'Major cities should ban private cars from their centers within ten years.'

Agent A: in favor.
Agent B: opposed.

--- Transcript so far ---
Moderator: Agent A, opening statement please.
Agent A: Private cars consume disproportionate urban space, harm air quality, and create traffic that punishes everyone, including drivers. A phased ban frees that space for transit, cycling, and street life that residents actually want.
Agent B: A ban penalizes the disabled, the elderly, and tradespeople who depend on vehicles. Public transit isn't ready in most cities, and you'd push activity to car-friendly suburbs, hollowing out the very centers you claim to protect.

DEBATE TOPIC: 'Remote work is better than in-office work for most knowledge workers.'

Agent A: in favor of remote.
Agent B: in favor of in-office.

--- Transcript so far ---
Agent A: Remote work returns commute hours to workers, broadens hiring beyond geography, and lets people structure their day around when they actually focus best. The output speaks for itself across many industries.
Agent B: Remote work erodes mentorship, slows the serendipitous collisions that produce real innovation, and isolates junior people who learn most by being around senior colleagues. Productivity numbers mask declining team cohesion.

DEBATE TOPIC: 'Frontier AI model weights should be released openly to the public.'

Agent A: in favor of open release.
Agent B: in favor of closed/gated release.

--- Transcript so far ---
Moderator: Agent A, opening.
Agent A: Open release lets researchers audit safety claims, enables economic competition outside a few labs, and concentrates fewer governance levers in private hands. The risks of closed systems are also real and worth pricing in.
Agent B: Open weights are non-recoverable. Once they're out, no one can take back misuse - and the marginal uplift to bad actors is exactly what closed labs argue is dangerous. The audit benefits don't require open weights; they require structured access.

DEBATE TOPIC: 'A universal basic income would do more good than harm in wealthy countries.'

Agent A: in favor.
Agent B: opposed.

--- Transcript so far ---
Agent A: A UBI delivers a floor of security that lets people negotiate, retrain, or start businesses without falling off a cliff. It replaces a maze of means-tested programs with something simple, dignified, and hard to game.
Agent B: A UBI either costs too much to be meaningful or, if generous, distorts labor markets and inflates the cost of essentials it was meant to cover. Targeted programs are clumsier but they actually move the people who need help the most.

DEBATE TOPIC: 'Eating meat is morally indefensible given modern alternatives.'

Agent A: in favor of the statement.
Agent B: opposed.

--- Transcript so far ---
Agent A: We now have alternatives that meet most nutritional needs without inflicting industrial-scale suffering on sentient animals. The moral asymmetry, our convenience versus their existence, doesn't survive examination.
Agent B: Many communities depend on animal agriculture economically and culturally; plant-only diets are not accessible everywhere; and the framing of all meat-eating as 'indefensible' lumps together very different practices. Reducing harm is a better goal than absolutism.

DEBATE TOPIC: 'Heritable human genetic engineering should be developed and made available to families with means.'

Agent A: in favor.
Agent B: opposed.

--- Transcript so far ---
Agent A: We routinely accept that parents make consequential decisions about their children's futures. Eliminating serious heritable disease, and later expanding to traits, is the next logical step in a long history of medicine and education improving children's odds.
Agent B: Heritable edits make irreversible decisions for future people who can't consent. Restricting access to families with means turns biology into a class system. The 'serious disease' framing slides quickly into trait selection, with no clear stopping point.
